channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Inkle co-founder explains how TR-49 broke even in three hours
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

April 14, 2026

3 Min Read

TR-49, the latest release from UK indie studio Inkle, broke even after just three hours.

That's according to Inkle co-founder Jon Ingold, who shared the news during a fireside chat at London Games Festival and suggested the title's low price point— six whole British pounds—was a factor in that near-instantaneous recoup.

There is, however, some vital context. Ingold stressed the budget for TR-49 was "very, very low" as a result of the project's minuscule asset requirement, narrative focus, and the fact production only lasted for nine months. "The biggest expense outside of the company was just a fair amount of voice acting," he explained.

In addition, Inkle has a substantial back catalog of titles such as 80 Days and Overboard! that mean the studio isn't completely reliant on income from new releases. Ingold and fellow co-founder Joseph Humfrey also don't factor their own salaries (and that of a single artist) into an individual title's budget, because it would still be paid out even if they took a break. 

Related:UK government commits £28.5 million to supporting local game developers

"We would be spending this money on ourselves anyway, so Joe and I cost the company zero. We're never going to stop paying our salaries under any circumstances because we need to eat," he added.

"If you accounted that way, we broke even in three hours or something, because it really didn't cost very much at all. Once you're a gentleman developer and you don't need to pay yourself, that's a pretty nice place to be. But that comes from this pattern of repeatedly reinvesting in our stuff—and occasionally you win and you try to blow it all on the next hand. We needed that security to be able to say we're going to charge a very low amount for this game." 

All of that being said, Ingold wonders whether we're witnessing a reckoning on platforms like Steam when it comes to consumer perceptions around pricing. It's a musing that comes in an era where low-cost releases like Peak and Content Warning—affectionately known as 'friendslop' titles—have generated millions of sales and taken social media by storm. 

"When 80 Days came out, it cost £3 a copy on mobile. Then we ported it to Steam. We were  looking at prices over there and we were like 'why is anybody paying this much? £25 for an indie game that might be okay? That's insane,'" continued Ingold. "So we priced it at £15 or something and we expected to get inundated by people saying 'What the hell! This is five times more expensive on Steam? That just didn't happen because the culture of Steam was one where people just threw virtual money into this bank and bought Gabe Newell another yacht—whereas on their phones they were very scared of doing that. 

Related:Roblox is introducing age-based accounts for children and teens

"I wonder if maybe that's changing a bit and we're starting to feel that the money we spend on our Steam back catalog is real money that we could actually be spending on heating and food? We still don't want to spend any money on mobile—so maybe there's parity coming there. Maybe it's long overdue.

"If that's the case, the solution to making premium games on mobile is to make them incredibly cheap. There's no other way to solve that problem. It's very difficult market to be profitable in, and I suspect that's probably true of Steam too. So, for our next project, are we going to go bigger? Absolutely not. No. Are we going to go more expensive. Maybe. I feel we might have underpriced this one a little bit, but we'll find out."

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/inkle-co-founder-explains-how-tr-49-broke-even-in-three-hours
